item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish the article went more into detail about how exactly The Foundation recognized the different instances are smarter than the average fly (to each their own).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't care at all for it or about it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me sad in a weird way I can't quite explain. It's short but enjoyable enough to upvote. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this the insectoid Hawkman and Hawkwoman?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those two things are pretty cute, considering that they're bugs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this is really close to the article I just read, and doesn't do the same thing. The whole thing just feels slapdash and rapid-fire: this does this, then this, then this. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a nice article, but you might want to go back through and edit all those SCP- XXX s. There are a lot of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of good points, especially the last one. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, and have upvoted. I have a few nitpicks though:

+ See them
- Aaagh, now I can't unsee them

1) The containment procedures don't actually say how they're contained. "at Site-18, in a standard plastic insect viewing box" would be enough.

2) You always refer to them as "the objects" which sounded like they were non-living. Suggest using "the insects".

3) As Agent MacLeod said, I found the verb form changes disorienting. By the end of the first paragraph, I thought that they were dead, and wondered why this wasn't classed as Neutralized. I think it would help to mention that we're talking about multiple generations up-front, and perhaps moving the paragraph on discovery towards the end of the entry, after describing the behaviour seen in testing.

4)

SCP-2616 had long memory spans, and were capable of solving advanced problems, such as navigating mazes.

This rest of the entry suggests that only SCP-2616-A is capable of solving problems, and that -B is not. Query whether this sentence is necessary at all?

5)

During containment the objects preferred to be together.

This feels like too much of an intuitive leap for the scientific method. I suggest sticking to the description of behaviour later in the paragraph, and letting the reader draw their own conclusions.

6)

SCP-2616-A observed SCP-2616-B deposit its larvae and mimicked this behavior after an extended time period.

Shouldn't this be the other way around? In all of the other examples, -B learns from -A or -A protects -B. Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. You took a basic concept and made and interesting spin, and it is the perfect length.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, cute, very cute.
anyway my main issue is shouldn't it be euclid?

(seriously though, I want to shrink down and snuggle with those little sweeties as if it they were my rabbits.)

EDIT: now I melted… thanks m8

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aye. I'll see if I can mix things up. I take liberties with the format for story pacing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The verb form needs to be tightened up (you mix past and future tense), and I think the fact that the phenomena is passed down from generation to generation ought to be put earlier in the description.

As it is, right now, it's a bit confusing. I was still able to figure it out, but the issues I noted are keeping this at a no-vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The researcher became suspicious of their behavior when he attempted to kill SCP-2616-B with a newspaper, at which point SCP-2616-A forcefully pushed SCP-2616-B away from the impact.

[incoherent bawling]

You made me ship flies

what even

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Done for Zyn's challenge.

Please ruin this cute article for me.
– hide block

B doesn't like A.

B wasn't always a fly. B was formerly human. B is taught how to be a fly (and everything being a fly entails) each time it is born.
